text: Many peptide hormones adopt α-helical structures upon receptor binding, and the ability to detect these molecules with high sensitivity is critical for disease management. While de novo protein design has successfully produced high-affinity binders for structured proteins, designing interactions for short, flexible peptides with helical propensity has remained a significant challenge. Here, we present parametric generation and deep learning-based strategies to overcome this limitation. By extending RFdiffusion to accommodate flexible targets and implementing a "partial diffusion" process—which refines structural models through successive noising and denoising—we generated picomolar-affinity binders for several helical peptide targets. These binders were achieved both by optimizing existing designs and through completely de novo generation from random noise, without requiring experimental maturation. We demonstrate that these RFdiffusion-optimized designs enable the enrichment and mass spectrometry-based detection of parathyroid hormone (PTH) and glucagon from complex samples, and can be integrated into bioluminescent protein biosensors. This capacity to design binders for conformationally variable targets and to refine both natural and synthetic proteins through partial diffusion offers a powerful new toolset for proteomics and diagnostics.
